brief_description: A city of contradictions where 17th-century canal houses line waterways crossed by 1,500 bridges, world-class museums sit beside coffeeshops, and cyclists rule streets once dominated by merchants. Amsterdam blends Dutch Golden Age grandeur with progressive modern culture—it's as likely to inspire with Rembrandt and Vermeer as with cutting-edge design, craft beer, and some of Europe's most liberated nightlife.

sections.title: Why Amsterdam
sections.content: Amsterdam punches far above its weight. In a compact, bikeable city you'll find three world-class art museums, a UNESCO-listed canal ring, and a food scene that's evolved far beyond stamppot and herring. The Dutch approach—pragmatic, tolerant, design-obsessed—shapes everything from the architecture to the social attitudes. This is a city that works, where infrastructure serves people rather than cars, and where gezelligheid (cozy conviviality) is a way of life.

sections.title: The Neighborhoods
sections.content: The Canal Ring (Grachtengordel) is the UNESCO-protected heart—three concentric canals lined with gabled merchant houses. Jordaan, once working-class, now houses boutiques, galleries, and brown cafés in narrow streets. De Pijp is multicultural and young, anchored by the Albert Cuyp Market. Museum Quarter holds the Rijksmuseum, Van Gogh, and Stedelijk in a single plaza. Noord, across the IJ, is Amsterdam's Brooklyn—industrial spaces turned creative hubs, accessible by free ferry. The Red Light District (De Wallen) remains what it is, but the medieval architecture is genuinely stunning.

sections.title: Getting Around
sections.content: Cycling is not optional—it's how Amsterdam moves. Rent a bike and join the flow; within a day you'll navigate like a local. The tram network covers what pedaling doesn't. Walking the canal ring takes an hour end to end, and you should do it at least once at night when bridges glow and houseboats flicker. Never stand in bike lanes (locals will let you know). The GVB day pass covers trams, buses, and Metro. Taxis exist but are rarely necessary in a city this compact.

why_visit: Three world-class art museums within walking distance—Rijksmuseum, Van Gogh, and Stedelijk
UNESCO-listed 17th-century canal ring, one of Europe's most beautiful urban landscapes
Bike-first culture where cycling is the fastest, most practical way to explore
Progressive, tolerant attitude that shapes everything from coffeeshops to nightlife
Gezelligheid—the Dutch concept of cozy conviviality found in brown cafés and candlelit restaurants

practical_info.tipping: Not required; round up or add 5-10% for good service
practical_info.tap_water: Excellent and safe—Amsterdam's tap water is among Europe's best

seasonal_notes.spring: Tulip season (mid-April), King's Day madness (April 27), ideal cycling weather
seasonal_notes.summer: Long days, outdoor terraces, festivals, but peak tourist crowds
seasonal_notes.fall: Cozy season begins, museum-perfect weather, Amsterdam Dance Event
seasonal_notes.winter: Cold and dark but magical—ice skating, holiday lights, fewer tourists

best_time_to_visit.overall: April-May for tulips and King's Day, or September-October for mild weather and fewer crowds
best_time_to_visit.weather: May-September for warmest weather and outdoor living
best_time_to_visit.events: King's Day (April 27), Amsterdam Dance Event (October), Amsterdam Light Festival (winter)

Must Do.activity: Canal Ring Walk or Boat Tour
